title: The EBITDA Number You Report and the One a Buyer Actually Pays For
linkedin:
A seller hands over accounts showing $620,000 net profit. Three weeks later, the buyer's advisers return with $540,000. The multiple gets applied to the second number.

The gap is not sloppy accounting. It is normalisation, the process of stripping out one-off and owner-specific items to work out what a business would actually earn under new ownership.

Some add-backs survive scrutiny. A genuine one-off legal dispute, a redundancy tied to a single restructuring event, an insurance claim shortfall. If the same type of cost shows up more than once across the last three years, buyers stop treating it as an exception.

The adjustment that catches owners most often runs in the opposite direction to what they expect. Many owner-led businesses pay themselves below the market value of the role they perform. A buyer does not add that back as profit. They impute a market-rate replacement salary as a cost, which reduces earnings rather than increasing them.

Related-party rent gets the same treatment, adjusted to market either way. Discretionary bonuses paid every year regardless of the label attached to them get treated as ordinary remuneration, not a one-off.

The businesses that come through a sale process with the least friction are the ones that have already reconstructed their numbers on this basis before a buyer asks for it.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#BusinessValuation #FractionalCFO #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Quick one for anyone thinking about selling in the next few years: the profit figure on your accounts and the earnings figure a buyer actually pays a multiple on are rarely the same number.

One adjustment surprises almost every owner. If you have been paying yourself below the market rate for your own role, a buyer does not add that back as extra profit. They deduct a market-rate replacement cost instead, which pulls the number down, not up.

Have you ever worked out what your own numbers would look like after that kind of review?
google:
Something we see often when working with East Coast business owners on valuation: the profit figure in your management accounts is rarely the number a buyer ends up paying a multiple on.

Buyers rebuild your earnings on what is called a normalised basis, stripping out genuine one-off costs and adjusting related-party items like family salaries or rent paid to a connected entity back to market rate. One adjustment catches almost every owner off guard. If you have been paying yourself below market value for the work you do, a buyer treats that as an understated cost, not a profit add-back, and it reduces the earnings figure rather than increasing it.

The businesses that handle a sale process with the least friction are the ones that have already worked through this exercise well before a buyer asks for it.

If you are a Brisbane, Queensland, or wider East Coast business owner with a sale on the horizon, even a few years out, this is worth understanding now while there is time to act on it. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
